schema_notes: Second Rashidun caliph. Converted in 616 as a committed opponent of Islam; his conversion marked a turning point for the Meccan community. As caliph he oversaw the great conquests — but personally left Arabia only once, travelling north to receive the surrender of Jerusalem in 637.
sources: Ibn Ishaq, Sirat Rasul Allah (Guillaume tr.)
Wilferd Madelung, The Succession to Muhammad

journey.event: Born in Mecca to the Banu Adi clan of Quraysh. His father al-Khattab was known for severity. Umar learned to read — rare among the Quraysh — and became a trader and a powerful wrestler.
During the first years of Islam he was one of its fiercest opponents in Mecca. Traditions describe him seeking to kill the Prophet.

Converted to Islam in Mecca. The traditional account has him setting out to kill the Prophet, then discovering his own sister and her husband reading the Quran, and being overcome. His conversion emboldened the small Muslim community to pray openly at the Kaaba for the first time.
Made the Hijra to Medina. Tradition says that, unlike others who migrated secretly, he walked openly around the Kaaba before leaving and challenged any Quraysh who wished to stop him.
Fought at Badr. His maternal uncle al-As ibn Hisham was killed in the battle.
Fought at Uhud.
Fought at the Battle of the Trench.

At Hudaybiyyah he initially objected strongly to the treaty's terms, which he felt were humiliating. The Prophet calmed him; Umar later said he gave charity and freed slaves in expiation for his argumentativeness that day.
Present at the conquest of Khaybar.
Entered Mecca with the Prophet at the conquest.
Fought at Hunayn.
Accompanied the Prophet on the Tabuk expedition. Gave half of his wealth to fund the campaign — Abu Bakr famously gave all of his.
Present at the Farewell Pilgrimage.
At the Saqifa of Banu Sa'ida, after the Prophet's death, he personally gave the first public pledge of allegiance to Abu Bakr — a decisive intervention that established the first caliphate.
Chief advisor and judge under Abu Bakr. Urged him to compile the Quran into a single mushaf after the Battle of Yamama.
Became the second caliph on the death of Abu Bakr. Took the new title Amir al-Mu'minin, Commander of the Faithful.

From Medina directed the conquests of Syria and Iraq. Battle of Qadisiyya (636) broke Sasanian Persia; Battle of Yarmuk (636) broke Byzantine Syria. He himself stayed in Medina, walking the streets at night to inspect the welfare of the city's poor.

Set out from Medina for the only major journey of his caliphate, travelling north to receive the surrender of Jerusalem in person as the Patriarch Sophronius had demanded. Arrived first at Jabiya in the Golan, where Abu Ubayda, Khalid ibn al-Walid, and Yazid ibn Abi Sufyan met him with an escort.

Entered Jerusalem dressed in simple robes, on foot beside his servant who was riding the camel. Received the formal surrender of the city from Sophronius. Composed the Covenant of Umar guaranteeing Christian religious freedom and places of worship. Declined the Patriarch's invitation to pray in the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, fearing it would later be converted into a mosque. Stayed ten days.
Returned to Medina from Jerusalem.

Set out for Syria but halted at Sargh near the frontier on news of the Plague of Amwas devastating the Muslim armies and civilians in Syria. After consultation he turned back rather than enter a plague zone — his famous ruling that 'we flee from the decree of God to the decree of God'.
Back in Medina. The plague killed several senior companions in Syria including the commander Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah.

Continued to govern from Medina. Instituted the Islamic Hijri calendar, dating it from the Prophet's migration. Established the diwan — a system of stipends for Muslims based on precedence in Islam. Expanded the Prophet's Mosque.
Directed the Battle of Nahavand, called the 'Victory of Victories' — the final collapse of Sasanian organised resistance. The conquest of Egypt under Amr ibn al-As was completed in these years.

Stabbed at dawn while leading prayers at the Prophet's Mosque by Abu Lu'lu'a, a Persian Christian slave nursing a grievance. Lingered three days before dying. Buried beside the Prophet and Abu Bakr. Nominated a council of six to choose his successor.

confidence.disclosure: Umar's decade-long caliphate (634-644) is among the most documented in early Islam. Al-Tabari, al-Baladhuri's Futuh, and Ibn Sa'd provide detailed campaign chronology. The conquests of Damascus, Jerusalem, Egypt, and Persia are well dated.
